1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses after the death of the two sons of Aaron, when they offered before the LORD, and died;" The witnesses read the great Day of Atonement law as set after the death of Nadab and Abihu — a warning of how dreadful it is to draw near to God unbidden. The witnesses note that Gill takes it either immediately after their death, so that this chapter stands next in order to the tenth, or after the laws of uncleanness, that Aaron be cautioned lest he die as his sons died. The witnesses note that the death of the two sons (Lev. 10:1-2) is the dark occasion of these directions. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the occasion — after the death of Aaron's two sons; the warning — that Aaron come not as they came, lest he die; the need — that the daily sacrifices left much sin unexpiated, requiring a yearly atonement.

The grammatical commentators (Keil and Delitzsch) add that the former sacrifices and purifications did not suffice to complete the reconciliation between the holy God and a nation still involved in sin; many sins remained unacknowledged and unexpiated, so a yearly all-comprehending atonement was needed.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn preface to the Day of Atonement: The witnesses note the occasion: after the death of Aaron's two sons. The witnesses note the warning: that Aaron come not as they came, lest he die. The witnesses note the need: that the daily sacrifices left much sin unexpiated, requiring a yearly atonement. The expositors observe the occasion: after the death of Aaron's two sons. The expositors observe the warning: that Aaron come not as they came, lest he die. The expositors observe the need: that the daily sacrifices left much sin unexpiated, requiring a yearly atonement. The believer is taught to mark the occasion, the warning, the need, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the most solemn rite of the law was given in the shadow of judgment, teaching reverence in approaching God.
2:
"that he come not at all times into the holy place within the vail before the mercy seat... that he die not: for I will appear in the cloud upon the mercy seat." The witnesses note that the high priest might not enter the holiest at all times, but only on this one day, and that under peril of death, for God appeared in the cloud above the mercy seat. The witnesses note that Gill marks this as binding on all high priests in succession from Aaron. The witnesses note that the way into the holiest was not yet made manifest (Heb. 9:7-8), the veil still standing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the restriction — not at all times, but only on the Day of Atonement; the peril — that he die not, the holiness of God being deadly to the unprepared; the presence — for God appeared in the cloud upon the mercy seat.

The grammatical commentators (Keil and Delitzsch) note the force of "with this" (v.3): only with the appointed sacrifices, dress, purifications, and means of expiation might he enter the inmost division, the holy of holies; and they connect the cloud upon the mercy seat with God's gracious yet awful presence.

So the witnesses make the verse the strict guarding of the holiest: The witnesses note the restriction: not at all times, but only on the Day of Atonement. The witnesses note the peril: that he die not, the holiness of God being deadly to the unprepared. The witnesses note the presence: for God appeared in the cloud upon the mercy seat. The expositors observe the restriction: not at all times, but only on the Day of Atonement. The expositors observe the peril: that he die not, the holiness of God being deadly to the unprepared. The expositors observe the presence: for God appeared in the cloud upon the mercy seat. The believer is taught to mark the restriction, the peril, the presence, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that access to God under the law was rare, guarded, and through blood — the very thing Christ has now opened (Heb. 10:19-20).
3:
"Thus shall Aaron come into the holy place: with a young bullock for a sin offering, and a ram for a burnt offering." The witnesses note the two sacrifices with which Aaron came: a bullock for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering — and that this followed the daily morning sacrifice. The witnesses note that Gill sets this after he had offered the daily sacrifice and burned the incense and trimmed the lamps. The witnesses note that the bullock (Clarke) was a sin offering for himself, his family, and the whole priesthood, and the ram a burnt offering to signify their entire consecration to the ministry. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sin offering — a young bullock for himself, his house, and the priesthood; the burnt offering — a ram, signifying entire consecration to the ministry; the order — after the daily morning sacrifice was offered.

The grammatical commentators (Ellicott) read "thus" as "with this," that is, with the following sacrifices, ritual, and vestments; and note that during the morning service the high priest wore the golden vestments, which he changed for the white garments before the special ritual of the day.

So the witnesses make the verse the appointed approach: The witnesses note the sin offering: a young bullock for himself, his house, and the priesthood. The witnesses note the burnt offering: a ram, signifying entire consecration to the ministry. The witnesses note the order: after the daily morning sacrifice was offered. The expositors observe the sin offering: a young bullock for himself, his house, and the priesthood. The expositors observe the burnt offering: a ram, signifying entire consecration to the ministry. The expositors observe the order: after the daily morning sacrifice was offered. The believer is taught to mark the sin offering, the burnt offering, the order, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the priest came to God only by sacrifice — sin first put away, then the life wholly devoted — a shadow of the one offering of Christ.
4:
"He shall put on the holy linen coat... these are holy garments; therefore shall he wash his flesh in water, and so put them on." The witnesses note that on this day the high priest laid aside his golden robes of glory and put on plain white linen, washing his flesh — the dress of humiliation, not of splendour. The witnesses note that Gill observes the linen coat was such as he wore in common with the other priests. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch list the coat, breeches, girdle and mitre, all of linen, as the holy garments, requiring the washing of the flesh first. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the linen — the plain white coat, breeches, girdle and mitre, not the golden robes; the washing — that he wash his flesh in water before putting them on; the meaning — humiliation and purity rather than glory and pomp.

The witnesses agree these white garments mark the lowliness and purity proper to the day — the high priest appearing before God not in his pomp but in spotless simplicity, a figure of Christ who, ministering for us, took the form of a servant (Phil. 2:7).

So the witnesses make the verse the garments of the day: The witnesses note the linen: the plain white coat, breeches, girdle and mitre, not the golden robes. The witnesses note the washing: that he wash his flesh in water before putting them on. The witnesses note the meaning: humiliation and purity rather than glory and pomp. The expositors observe the linen: the plain white coat, breeches, girdle and mitre, not the golden robes. The expositors observe the washing: that he wash his flesh in water before putting them on. The expositors observe the meaning: humiliation and purity rather than glory and pomp. The believer is taught to mark the linen, the washing, the meaning, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that he who would atone must appear before God in purity and lowliness — Christ our High Priest clothed not in earthly splendour but in spotless righteousness.
5:
"And he shall take of the congregation of the children of Israel two kids of the goats for a sin offering, and one ram for a burnt offering." The witnesses note the people's offering: two goats for a sin offering and one ram for a burnt offering, taken of the congregation. The witnesses note that Gill sees in this that the high priest had to do, on this day, only with the Israel of God — as Christ our High Priest with the elect given him by the Father, for whom he offered himself and made reconciliation. The witnesses note that the two goats together form the single sin offering for the people (vv.7-10). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sin offering — two goats taken of the congregation; the burnt offering — one ram, the people's entire devotion; the preparation — the high priest sequestered seven days beforehand.

The puritan expositor (Henry) adds the tradition that for seven days before, the high priest withdrew to a chamber of the temple to prepare himself, doing the inferior priests' work and having the institution read over to him again and again, that he might be fully apprised of the whole method of so great a day.

So the witnesses make the verse the people's sacrifice: The witnesses note the sin offering: two goats taken of the congregation. The witnesses note the burnt offering: one ram, the people's entire devotion. The witnesses note the preparation: the high priest sequestered seven days beforehand. The expositors observe the sin offering: two goats taken of the congregation. The expositors observe the burnt offering: one ram, the people's entire devotion. The expositors observe the preparation: the high priest sequestered seven days beforehand. The believer is taught to mark the sin offering, the burnt offering, the preparation, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the high priest acted for the whole people — a figure of Christ, who has to do with the Israel of God and offered himself for them.
6:
"And Aaron shall offer his bullock of the sin offering, which is for himself, and make an atonement for himself, and for his house." The witnesses note that Aaron must first atone for himself and his own house before he could atone for the people — for he was a sinner like them. The witnesses note that Gill explains "for himself" as at his own expense and for his own family, the bullock being wholly of his own money, not the people's. The witnesses note that the high priest's own sins had first to be expiated (Heb. 5:3; Heb. 9:7) before he could offer for others. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the for himself — the bullock at his own expense, for his own sin; the for his house — that his family and the priesthood be covered first; the contrast — that Christ, sinless, needed no such offering for himself.

The witnesses with one voice mark here the great contrast with Christ: the Aaronic priest needed an offering for his own sin first, but our High Priest, holy and undefiled, needed no atonement for himself and so could offer once for all (Heb. 7:27).

So the witnesses make the verse the priest's own atonement: The witnesses note the for himself: the bullock at his own expense, for his own sin. The witnesses note the for his house: that his family and the priesthood be covered first. The witnesses note the contrast: that Christ, sinless, needed no such offering for himself. The expositors observe the for himself: the bullock at his own expense, for his own sin. The expositors observe the for his house: that his family and the priesthood be covered first. The expositors observe the contrast: that Christ, sinless, needed no such offering for himself. The believer is taught to mark the for himself, the for his house, the contrast, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the law's priest must atone for his own sin before the people's — the very weakness that magnifies the sinless High Priest who has no need to offer for himself.
7:
"And he shall take the two goats, and present them before the LORD at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation." The witnesses note the two goats set before the LORD at the door — together one sin offering, yet about to be divided by lot. The witnesses note that Gill reads the two goats as a single emblem of Christ: the clean creature fit for food denoting his purity and his being wholesome food to faith, and the number two typifying either the two natures, or his death and his risen life. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note both were presented at the door before lots were cast. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the one offering — two goats forming a single sin offering for the people; the the clean creature — fit for food, an emblem of Christ's purity; the the number two — figuring his two natures, or his death and risen life.

The witnesses agree the two goats make one offering with two sides — the one slain, the one sent away — that together they might set forth both the death that pays for sin and the carrying-away of sin out of sight, which no single creature could picture at once.

So the witnesses make the verse the two goats: The witnesses note the one offering: two goats forming a single sin offering for the people. The witnesses note the the clean creature: fit for food, an emblem of Christ's purity. The witnesses note the the number two: figuring his two natures, or his death and risen life. The expositors observe the one offering: two goats forming a single sin offering for the people. The expositors observe the the clean creature: fit for food, an emblem of Christ's purity. The expositors observe the the number two: figuring his two natures, or his death and risen life. The believer is taught to mark the one offering, the the clean creature, the the number two, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that one creature could not show forth the whole of redemption, so two goats were taken — the one to die, the other to bear sin away.
8:
"And Aaron shall cast lots upon the two goats; one lot for the LORD, and the other lot for the scapegoat." The witnesses note the casting of lots that divided the two goats: one "for the LORD," to be slain, and one "for Azazel," the scapegoat, to be sent away. The witnesses note that Gill details the rabbinic account of the urn (the Calphi) from which the high priest drew the two lots inscribed "for the LORD" and "for Azazel." The witnesses note that Clarke reads the Hebrew of the second lot, la-azazel, "for the scape-goat," distinguishing it from the goat that was offered. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the for the LORD — the goat to be slain as a sin offering; the for Azazel — the goat to be sent away alive into the wilderness; the the name — disputed — "the goat that departs," a place, or an evil spirit.

Here the witnesses divide over the name Azazel: the grammatical and Wesleyan readers (Clarke) take it as a common noun, "the goat that departs," the dismissed or sent-away goat; while others, with Ellicott, take it of a place in the wilderness, or even of an evil spirit dwelling there — though all agree the rite itself signifies sin carried clean away.

So the witnesses make the verse the lots: The witnesses note the for the LORD: the goat to be slain as a sin offering. The witnesses note the for Azazel: the goat to be sent away alive into the wilderness. The witnesses note the the name: disputed — "the goat that departs," a place, or an evil spirit. The expositors observe the for the LORD: the goat to be slain as a sin offering. The expositors observe the for Azazel: the goat to be sent away alive into the wilderness. The expositors observe the the name: disputed — "the goat that departs," a place, or an evil spirit. The believer is taught to mark the for the LORD, the for Azazel, the the name, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the lot, not man's choosing, parted the goats — God himself appointing which should die and which bear sin away.
9:
"And Aaron shall bring the goat upon which the LORD's lot fell, and offer him for a sin offering." The witnesses note that the goat on which the LORD's lot fell was brought and offered for a sin offering — slain, its blood carried within the veil. The witnesses note that Gill describes how the high priest, taking out the lot, lifted it up, then laid it on the head of the goat and brought it to the altar. The witnesses note that this slain goat is the type of Christ offered for sin (v.15). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the chosen by lot — the goat on which the LORD's lot fell; the the sin offering — brought to be slain for the people; the the type — of Christ offered for sin, his blood carried within the veil.

The grammatical commentator (Ellicott) notes that immediately after the lots fell, the goats were distinguished by marks — a red thread tied on the one for Jehovah — and that "offer" here means appoint or present, the actual offering taking place afterward.

So the witnesses make the verse the Lord's goat: The witnesses note the chosen by lot: the goat on which the LORD's lot fell. The witnesses note the the sin offering: brought to be slain for the people. The witnesses note the the type: of Christ offered for sin, his blood carried within the veil. The expositors observe the chosen by lot: the goat on which the LORD's lot fell. The expositors observe the the sin offering: brought to be slain for the people. The expositors observe the the type: of Christ offered for sin, his blood carried within the veil. The believer is taught to mark the chosen by lot, the the sin offering, the the type, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the goat marked out for the LORD was the one to die — a figure of Christ, set apart by God to be the sin offering of his people.
10:
"But the goat on which the lot fell to be the scapegoat shall be presented alive before the LORD, to make an atonement with him, and to let him go for a scapegoat into the wilderness." The witnesses note the second goat, presented alive, not slain, to make atonement by being sent away into the wilderness bearing the people's sin. The witnesses note that Gill defers the question of Azazel, treating the scapegoat as a distinct figure. The witnesses note that Clarke derives azazel from "goat" and "to dismiss," the dismissed or sent-away goat, and compares vicarious rites among the nations whereby a community's guilt was transferred to a victim. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the presented alive — not slain, but set living before the LORD; the the atonement — made by sending him away, sin carried off; the the wilderness — a land not inhabited, sin removed out of sight.

The witnesses again divide over Azazel and the live goat's meaning, yet agree the living goat completes what the slain goat began: the one shows atonement made by death, the other sin carried clean away and gone out of sight — together the whole of what Christ accomplished, dying for sin and bearing it away.

So the witnesses make the verse the living goat: The witnesses note the presented alive: not slain, but set living before the LORD. The witnesses note the the atonement: made by sending him away, sin carried off. The witnesses note the the wilderness: a land not inhabited, sin removed out of sight. The expositors observe the presented alive: not slain, but set living before the LORD. The expositors observe the the atonement: made by sending him away, sin carried off. The expositors observe the the wilderness: a land not inhabited, sin removed out of sight. The believer is taught to mark the presented alive, the the atonement, the the wilderness, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that atonement is two-sided — sin paid for by death, and sin borne away — the live goat picturing the second half of the one work of Christ.
11:
"And Aaron shall bring the bullock of the sin offering, which is for himself, and shall make an atonement for himself, and for his house, and shall kill the bullock of the sin offering which is for himself." The witnesses note that Aaron, having presented the goats and cast the lots, returns to his own bullock, again confessing his sin and his house's, and kills it. The witnesses note that Gill takes this in the same sense as v.6 — the bullock for himself. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes he laid both hands a second time on the victim and repeated the confession for himself, his house, and the whole priesthood, his own sin first expiated (Heb. 9:7) before he could atone for the people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the for himself — the sin offering for the priest and his house; the the confession — both hands laid on, sin confessed a second time; the the order — his own sin first, then he may atone for the people.

The witnesses agree the repetition is no idle ceremony but the law's insistence that the mediator must himself be cleansed before he can cleanse others — a need that throws into relief the sinless Mediator who had none.

So the witnesses make the verse the bullock slain: The witnesses note the for himself: the sin offering for the priest and his house. The witnesses note the the confession: both hands laid on, sin confessed a second time. The witnesses note the the order: his own sin first, then he may atone for the people. The expositors observe the for himself: the sin offering for the priest and his house. The expositors observe the the confession: both hands laid on, sin confessed a second time. The expositors observe the the order: his own sin first, then he may atone for the people. The believer is taught to mark the for himself, the the confession, the the order, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the law's priest must be cleansed before he cleanses — the very weakness Hebrews sets against the spotless Son who needs no such offering.
12:
"And he shall take a censer full of burning coals of fire from off the altar... and his hands full of sweet incense beaten small, and bring it within the vail:" The witnesses note the golden censer of coals and the handful of sweet incense carried within the veil — the cloud of incense going up before the blood. The witnesses note that Gill notes the golden censer used on this day, and sees in it Christ the Angel of God's presence, our interceding High Priest (Rev. 8:3). The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch join vv.12-15, the incense and the blood, as the inmost service. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the golden censer — full of burning coals from off the altar; the the sweet incense — beaten small, a handful brought within the veil; the the meaning — the sweet odour of Christ's intercession ascending before God.

The witnesses agree the incense going up within the veil before the blood was sprinkled figures the sweet odour of Christ's intercession and merit, ascending before God to cover the mercy seat — the prayer and worth of the Mediator going up with the atoning blood.

So the witnesses make the verse the incense within the veil: The witnesses note the the golden censer: full of burning coals from off the altar. The witnesses note the the sweet incense: beaten small, a handful brought within the veil. The witnesses note the the meaning: the sweet odour of Christ's intercession ascending before God. The expositors observe the the golden censer: full of burning coals from off the altar. The expositors observe the the sweet incense: beaten small, a handful brought within the veil. The expositors observe the the meaning: the sweet odour of Christ's intercession ascending before God. The believer is taught to mark the the golden censer, the the sweet incense, the the meaning, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the incense went up before the blood was sprinkled — the merit and intercession of the High Priest covering the place where atonement is made.
13:
"And he shall put the incense upon the fire before the LORD, that the cloud of the incense may cover the mercy seat that is upon the testimony, that he die not:" The witnesses note the incense laid on the coals before the LORD, the cloud rising to cover the mercy seat — without which screen the priest would die. The witnesses note that Gill stresses, against the Sadducees, that the incense was kindled within the veil, not without, the high priest being sworn to make no alteration. The witnesses note that the covering cloud guards the priest from the unveiled glory of God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the within the veil — the incense kindled before the LORD, not without; the the screen — the cloud covering the mercy seat, that he die not; the the figure — christ's merit veiling the sinner from consuming holiness.

The witnesses agree the cloud of incense is the merciful screen between the sinner's mediator and the consuming holiness of God — a figure of the intercession and worth of Christ, through which alone the believer is preserved alive in God's presence.

So the witnesses make the verse the covering cloud: The witnesses note the within the veil: the incense kindled before the LORD, not without. The witnesses note the the screen: the cloud covering the mercy seat, that he die not. The witnesses note the the figure: Christ's merit veiling the sinner from consuming holiness. The expositors observe the within the veil: the incense kindled before the LORD, not without. The expositors observe the the screen: the cloud covering the mercy seat, that he die not. The expositors observe the the figure: Christ's merit veiling the sinner from consuming holiness. The believer is taught to mark the within the veil, the the screen, the the figure, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that no man may stand in the unveiled presence of God and live, save under the covering cloud — the intercession and worth of the great High Priest.
14:
"And he shall take of the blood of the bullock, and sprinkle it with his finger upon the mercy seat eastward; and before the mercy seat shall he sprinkle of the blood with his finger seven times." The witnesses note the blood of the bullock sprinkled with the finger upon and before the mercy seat, seven times — atoning blood brought to the very throne of God. The witnesses note that Gill describes the blood kept stirred lest it congeal, and carried in a second time after the incense. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the high priest sprinkled once upward and seven times downward, counting each lest he err. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the on the mercy seat — the bullock's blood applied with the finger eastward; the seven times — the perfect, complete sprinkling before the mercy seat; the the meaning — that God is merciful to sinners through atoning blood.

The Wesleyan reader (Wesley) draws the heart of it: the blood upon the mercy seat teaches that God is merciful to sinners only through the blood of atonement — the mercy seat made a throne of grace because sprinkled blood is there.

So the witnesses make the verse the sprinkled blood: The witnesses note the on the mercy seat: the bullock's blood applied with the finger eastward. The witnesses note the seven times: the perfect, complete sprinkling before the mercy seat. The witnesses note the the meaning: that God is merciful to sinners through atoning blood. The expositors observe the on the mercy seat: the bullock's blood applied with the finger eastward. The expositors observe the seven times: the perfect, complete sprinkling before the mercy seat. The expositors observe the the meaning: that God is merciful to sinners through atoning blood. The believer is taught to mark the on the mercy seat, the seven times, the the meaning, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the mercy seat becomes a throne of grace only where the blood is sprinkled — God merciful to sinners through the atonement, fulfilled in Christ the propitiation (Rom. 3:25).
15:
"Then shall he kill the goat of the sin offering that is for the people, and bring his blood within the vail... and sprinkle it upon the mercy seat, and before the mercy seat:" The witnesses note the goat for the people now slain, its blood carried within the veil and sprinkled like the bullock's — atonement made for the people themselves. The witnesses note that Gill sees the slain goat as the type of Christ, made an offering for the sins of his people. The witnesses note that Henry traces the priest going a third time into the holiest, to sprinkle the goat's blood and so make atonement for the holy place defiled by the people's sins. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the goat slain — the sin offering for the people killed; the the blood within — carried within the veil, sprinkled as the bullock's; the the type — of Christ entering by his own blood into the holiest.

The witnesses agree this is the heart of the day: not the priest's sin only but the people's is now covered, the goat's blood brought where no Israelite might come — a figure of Christ entering by his own blood into the holiest of all (Heb. 9:12).

So the witnesses make the verse the people's atonement: The witnesses note the the goat slain: the sin offering for the people killed. The witnesses note the the blood within: carried within the veil, sprinkled as the bullock's. The witnesses note the the type: of Christ entering by his own blood into the holiest. The expositors observe the the goat slain: the sin offering for the people killed. The expositors observe the the blood within: carried within the veil, sprinkled as the bullock's. The expositors observe the the type: of Christ entering by his own blood into the holiest. The believer is taught to mark the the goat slain, the the blood within, the the type, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the people's sin was covered by blood brought into the very presence of God — fulfilled when Christ entered once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption.
16:
"And he shall make an atonement for the holy place, because of the uncleanness of the children of Israel, and because of their transgressions in all their sins:" The witnesses note that atonement was made even for the holy place and the tabernacle, defiled by the people's uncleanness while it stood in their midst. The witnesses note that Gill takes the holy place here as the holy of holies, atonement made by the sprinkled blood. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch explain the holy things were defiled by the bodily manifestations of the nation's sin, and so themselves required a yearly cleansing by the blood of sacrifice. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the holy place — the holy of holies, atoned for by blood; the the defilement — the uncleanness and transgressions of the people; the the reason — the holy things polluted by the nation's sin while it dwelt among them.

The witnesses agree the lesson is searching: even the place of God's dwelling, set among a sinful people, contracted defilement and needed atoning blood — so deep does sin reach, and so wide must the cleansing be that touches even the sanctuary.

So the witnesses make the verse the cleansed sanctuary: The witnesses note the the holy place: the holy of holies, atoned for by blood. The witnesses note the the defilement: the uncleanness and transgressions of the people. The witnesses note the the reason: the holy things polluted by the nation's sin while it dwelt among them. The expositors observe the the holy place: the holy of holies, atoned for by blood. The expositors observe the the defilement: the uncleanness and transgressions of the people. The expositors observe the the reason: the holy things polluted by the nation's sin while it dwelt among them. The believer is taught to mark the the holy place, the the defilement, the the reason, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that sin defiles even the place of worship, so that the sanctuary itself needed atonement — pointing to the heavenly things purified by better sacrifices (Heb. 9:23).
17:
"And there shall be no man in the tabernacle of the congregation when he goeth in to make an atonement in the holy place, until he come out..." The witnesses note that no man might be in the tabernacle while the high priest made atonement — he must do it utterly alone. The witnesses note that Gill stresses that all places were cleared, not a priest nor any of the people remaining. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch give the reason: no unholy person was to defile by his presence the sanctuary just cleansed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no man present — the tabernacle and courts cleared of all, not a priest nor any of the people remaining; the the reason — that no unholy presence defile the cleansed sanctuary just made holy by blood; the the solitude — the great atonement wrought by one Mediator, without helper; the the type — of Christ atoning alone, who of the people had none with him.

The witnesses agree this solitary act is among the clearest shadows of Christ, who trod the winepress alone and of the people there was none with him (Isa. 63:3); the great atonement was wrought by one Mediator, without helper, the people shut out till he came forth.

So the witnesses make the verse the priest alone: The witnesses note the no man present: the tabernacle and courts cleared of all, not a priest nor any of the people remaining. The witnesses note the the reason: that no unholy presence defile the cleansed sanctuary just made holy by blood. The witnesses note the the solitude: the great atonement wrought by one Mediator, without helper. The witnesses note the the type: of Christ atoning alone, who of the people had none with him. The expositors observe the no man present: the tabernacle and courts cleared of all, not a priest nor any of the people remaining. The expositors observe the the reason: that no unholy presence defile the cleansed sanctuary just made holy by blood. The expositors observe the the solitude: the great atonement wrought by one Mediator, without helper. The expositors observe the the type: of Christ atoning alone, who of the people had none with him. The believer is taught to mark the no man present, the the reason, the the solitude, the the type, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that atonement was the work of one man alone — a figure of the one Mediator who accomplished redemption by himself, the people waiting till he came out.
18:
"And he shall go out unto the altar that is before the LORD, and make an atonement for it; and shall take of the blood of the bullock, and of the blood of the goat, and put it upon the horns of the altar round about." The witnesses note the high priest now coming out to the altar, atoning for it with the mingled blood of bullock and goat put upon its horns round about. The witnesses note that Gill takes this of the golden altar of incense, on whose horns atonement was made once a year (Exod. 30:10). The witnesses note that the blood of both victims was mingled and applied together. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the altar — the place of meeting, atoned for by blood; the the mingled blood — of the bullock and of the goat together; the the horns — blood put upon the horns of the altar round about.

The grammatical readers note a genuine question whether the altar here is the golden altar of incense within, or the brazen altar of burnt offering without; Gill and the Mishnah hold the golden, while others (Aben Ezra, Bishop Patrick) urge the brazen, lest no cleansing be provided for it — but all agree the altar itself, where Israel met God, needed atoning blood.

So the witnesses make the verse the cleansing of the altar: The witnesses note the the altar: the place of meeting, atoned for by blood. The witnesses note the the mingled blood: of the bullock and of the goat together. The witnesses note the the horns: blood put upon the horns of the altar round about. The expositors observe the the altar: the place of meeting, atoned for by blood. The expositors observe the the mingled blood: of the bullock and of the goat together. The expositors observe the the horns: blood put upon the horns of the altar round about. The believer is taught to mark the the altar, the the mingled blood, the the horns, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that even the altar must be cleansed by blood — nothing in the worship of God being fit for use until the atonement has touched it.
19:
"And he shall sprinkle of the blood upon it with his finger seven times, and cleanse it, and hallow it from the uncleanness of the children of Israel." The witnesses note the seven sprinklings of blood upon the altar, both to cleanse it and to hallow it from the uncleanness of the people. The witnesses note that Gill notes the sprinkling was done with the right forefinger, seven times, to denote the perfect cleansing of the altar. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the double end — to cleanse and to hallow — the altar made fit again for holy use. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seven times — the blood sprinkled with the finger, the number of completeness; the cleanse — the altar purged of the people's defilement; the hallow — the altar set apart again for holy use; the the figure — the blood perfecting what it cleanses, as the blood of Christ perfects his people.

The witnesses agree the seven sprinklings mark a complete and perfect cleansing, the sacred number teaching that the work left nothing of the people's defilement upon the holy things — a shadow of the blood that perfects what it cleanses (Heb. 10:14).

So the witnesses make the verse the perfect cleansing: The witnesses note the seven times: the blood sprinkled with the finger, the number of completeness. The witnesses note the cleanse: the altar purged of the people's defilement. The witnesses note the hallow: the altar set apart again for holy use. The witnesses note the the figure: the blood perfecting what it cleanses, as the blood of Christ perfects his people. The expositors observe the seven times: the blood sprinkled with the finger, the number of completeness. The expositors observe the cleanse: the altar purged of the people's defilement. The expositors observe the hallow: the altar set apart again for holy use. The expositors observe the the figure: the blood perfecting what it cleanses, as the blood of Christ perfects his people. The believer is taught to mark the seven times, the cleanse, the hallow, the the figure, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the blood both cleanses and sanctifies, leaving the holy things perfectly purged — the very twofold work the blood of Christ accomplishes for his people.
20:
"And when he hath made an end of reconciling the holy place, and the tabernacle of the congregation, and the altar, he shall bring the live goat:" The witnesses note that only when the reconciling of the holy place, tabernacle, and altar was finished did the high priest bring forward the live goat. The witnesses note that Gill takes the reconciling of the holy place as the carrying-in and sprinkling of the blood of bullock and goat. The witnesses note that Henry sees the order: the blood being presented, the remainder poured out at the foot of the brazen altar, the priest is next to confess Israel's sins over the scapegoat. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the reconciling ended — the holy place, tabernacle, and altar cleansed by blood; the then the live goat — brought forward only when the blood-work was done; the the meaning — sin borne away resting on atonement already made.

The witnesses agree the sequence is significant: the blood-work within comes first and is finished, then the bearing-away of sin without; atonement made and accepted is the ground on which the sins are then carried off — exactly the order of the gospel, pardon flowing from a finished sacrifice.

So the witnesses make the verse the order of the day: The witnesses note the reconciling ended: the holy place, tabernacle, and altar cleansed by blood. The witnesses note the then the live goat: brought forward only when the blood-work was done. The witnesses note the the meaning: sin borne away resting on atonement already made. The expositors observe the reconciling ended: the holy place, tabernacle, and altar cleansed by blood. The expositors observe the then the live goat: brought forward only when the blood-work was done. The expositors observe the the meaning: sin borne away resting on atonement already made. The believer is taught to mark the reconciling ended, the then the live goat, the the meaning, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the carrying-away of sin follows a finished atonement — the live goat brought only after the blood had done its work, as pardon follows the cross.
21:
"And Aaron shall lay both his hands upon the head of the live goat, and confess over him all the iniquities of the children of Israel... putting them upon the head of the goat, and shall send him away by the hand of a fit man into the wilderness:" The witnesses note the laying of both hands on the live goat with confession of all Israel's sins, transferring them to its head, and the sending away by a fit man. The witnesses note that Gill reads the laying of both hands, the right upon the left, as transferring the people's sins and their punishment to the goat. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the confession of all the iniquities, transgressions, and sins, laid upon the head and borne into the wilderness by an appointed man. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the both hands laid on — transferring the people's sins to the goat's head; the confession — all the iniquities, transgressions, and sins acknowledged; the sent away — borne off by a fit man into the wilderness.

The witnesses agree this is the clearest picture in the law of the imputation of sin to a substitute: by confession and the laying-on of hands the guilt of the people is reckoned to the goat, which carries it away — the shadow of him on whom the LORD laid the iniquity of us all (Isa. 53:6).

So the witnesses make the verse the transfer of sin: The witnesses note the both hands laid on: transferring the people's sins to the goat's head. The witnesses note the confession: all the iniquities, transgressions, and sins acknowledged. The witnesses note the sent away: borne off by a fit man into the wilderness. The expositors observe the both hands laid on: transferring the people's sins to the goat's head. The expositors observe the confession: all the iniquities, transgressions, and sins acknowledged. The expositors observe the sent away: borne off by a fit man into the wilderness. The believer is taught to mark the both hands laid on, the confession, the sent away, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that sin was confessed and laid upon a living substitute to be carried away — the very figure of the LORD laying our iniquity upon Christ, who bore it from us.
22:
"And the goat shall bear upon him all their iniquities unto a land not inhabited: and he shall let go the goat in the wilderness." The witnesses note the goat bearing all the people's iniquities to a land not inhabited, let go where it would never be seen nor return. The witnesses note that Gill makes it a proper type of Christ, who bore the sins of his people in his own body, removed them as far as the east is from the west, out of the sight of avenging justice. The witnesses note that Ellicott renders "a land cut off," a place separated from all around, and notes the later temple practice of destroying the goat at the rock Zuck. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bearing iniquities — the goat carrying all the people's sins; the a land not inhabited — a place cut off, where sin is never seen again; the let go — sin sent away, never to return upon the people.

The witnesses agree the carrying-away pictures the entire removal of sin: not merely covered but borne off, gone, never to return upon the people — the gospel's assurance that pardoned sin is sought and not found, cast behind God's back and into the depths of the sea (Mic. 7:19).

So the witnesses make the verse the removal of sin: The witnesses note the bearing iniquities: the goat carrying all the people's sins. The witnesses note the a land not inhabited: a place cut off, where sin is never seen again. The witnesses note the let go: sin sent away, never to return upon the people. The expositors observe the bearing iniquities: the goat carrying all the people's sins. The expositors observe the a land not inhabited: a place cut off, where sin is never seen again. The expositors observe the let go: sin sent away, never to return upon the people. The believer is taught to mark the bearing iniquities, the a land not inhabited, the let go, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that sin is not merely covered but carried clean away, gone out of sight and never returning — fulfilled in Christ who put away sin by the sacrifice of himself.
23:
"And Aaron shall come into the tabernacle of the congregation, and shall put off the linen garments... and shall leave them there:" The witnesses note Aaron returning into the tabernacle to put off the white linen garments and leave them there, the special service of the day being ended. The witnesses note that Gill relays the rabbinic view that this was after the burnt offerings, and that the order of the verse is not strictly its place in time. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the linen garments were worn only for the expiatory ritual and then laid aside. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the return — aaron coming again into the tabernacle; the put off — the white linen garments removed; the left there — the garments laid up, worn only for this service; the the meaning — the lowliness assumed for the one purpose of putting away sin.

The witnesses agree the laying-aside of the linen marks the close of the unique atoning service; the white garments, worn for this one work, were left in the holy place — a small token that the day's humiliation belonged to the atonement alone.

So the witnesses make the verse the laid-aside linen: The witnesses note the return: Aaron coming again into the tabernacle. The witnesses note the put off: the white linen garments removed. The witnesses note the left there: the garments laid up, worn only for this service. The witnesses note the the meaning: the lowliness assumed for the one purpose of putting away sin. The expositors observe the return: Aaron coming again into the tabernacle. The expositors observe the put off: the white linen garments removed. The expositors observe the left there: the garments laid up, worn only for this service. The expositors observe the the meaning: the lowliness assumed for the one purpose of putting away sin. The believer is taught to mark the return, the put off, the left there, the the meaning, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the garments of humiliation belonged to the atoning work alone, laid aside when it was done — the lowliness assumed for the one purpose of putting away sin.
24:
"And he shall wash his flesh with water in the holy place, and put on his garments, and come forth, and offer his burnt offering, and the burnt offering of the people, and make an atonement for himself, and for the people." The witnesses note Aaron washing, resuming his garments of glory, and offering the burnt offerings for himself and the people. The witnesses note that Gill describes the washing as the dipping of the whole body, and ventures that if our Lord was baptized on this day there is a likeness of type and antitype. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note he washed, put on his (golden) garments, and offered the burnt offerings, making atonement. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the washing — the whole body dipped in the holy place; the resumed garments — the priest clothed again in his robes; the burnt offerings — for himself and the people, the cleansed wholly devoted; the the order — guilt removed by the sin offering, then the worshipper wholly given to God.

The witnesses agree the burnt offerings now consummate the day: sin having been expiated, priest and people are presented to God in entire devotion; the sin offering removes guilt, the burnt offering offers the cleansed worshipper wholly to God — the gospel order of pardon issuing in consecration.

So the witnesses make the verse the consummating offerings: The witnesses note the washing: the whole body dipped in the holy place. The witnesses note the resumed garments: the priest clothed again in his robes. The witnesses note the burnt offerings: for himself and the people, the cleansed wholly devoted. The witnesses note the the order: guilt removed by the sin offering, then the worshipper wholly given to God. The expositors observe the washing: the whole body dipped in the holy place. The expositors observe the resumed garments: the priest clothed again in his robes. The expositors observe the burnt offerings: for himself and the people, the cleansed wholly devoted. The expositors observe the the order: guilt removed by the sin offering, then the worshipper wholly given to God. The believer is taught to mark the washing, the resumed garments, the burnt offerings, the the order, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that atonement issues in entire devotion — guilt removed by the sin offering, the worshipper then wholly given to God in the burnt offering.
25:
"And the fat of the sin offering shall he burn upon the altar." The witnesses note the fat of the sin offerings burned upon the brazen altar, the choicest part given wholly to God. The witnesses note that Gill, with Jarchi, takes this of the fat on the inwards of both the bullock and the goat, burned at the same time as the burnt offerings. The witnesses note that Ellicott likewise reads the fat of both the bullock and the goat that made the sin offering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the fat — of the inwards of both bullock and goat; the the altar — burned upon the brazen altar of burnt offering; the God's portion — the choicest part given wholly to him; the the lesson — that atonement ends not in bare relief but in honour rendered to God.

The witnesses agree the burning of the fat marks God's portion even in the sin offering — the best reserved for him; so even where sin is dealt with, the LORD claims the choicest, teaching that atonement is no bare transaction but ends in honour rendered to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the fat burned: The witnesses note the the fat: of the inwards of both bullock and goat. The witnesses note the the altar: burned upon the brazen altar of burnt offering. The witnesses note the God's portion: the choicest part given wholly to him. The witnesses note the the lesson: that atonement ends not in bare relief but in honour rendered to God. The expositors observe the the fat: of the inwards of both bullock and goat. The expositors observe the the altar: burned upon the brazen altar of burnt offering. The expositors observe the God's portion: the choicest part given wholly to him. The expositors observe the the lesson: that atonement ends not in bare relief but in honour rendered to God. The believer is taught to mark the the fat, the the altar, the God's portion, the the lesson, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that even in the sin offering God claims the choicest, the fat burned to him — atonement ending not in bare relief but in honour rendered to God.
26:
"And he that let go the goat for the scapegoat shall wash his clothes, and bathe his flesh in water, and afterward he shall come into the camp." The witnesses note that the man who led away the scapegoat was himself rendered unclean and must wash before returning to the camp. The witnesses note that Gill again distinguishes the goat from Azazel, noting these are not the same. The witnesses note that Clarke explains the man was reckoned unclean because the goat itself was unclean, bearing the sins of the whole congregation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the man — he that led away the scapegoat; the made unclean — because the goat bore the sins of the congregation; the washing — required before he could come into the camp; the the lesson — the real weight of guilt laid upon the substitute who was made sin for us.

The witnesses agree the defilement of the man who handled the sin-laden goat shows how truly the people's guilt rested on it — whatever touched the bearer of sin contracted its uncleanness; a solemn token of the weight of the iniquity laid upon the substitute.

So the witnesses make the verse the defiled bearer: The witnesses note the the man: he that led away the scapegoat. The witnesses note the made unclean: because the goat bore the sins of the congregation. The witnesses note the washing: required before he could come into the camp. The witnesses note the the lesson: the real weight of guilt laid upon the substitute who was made sin for us. The expositors observe the the man: he that led away the scapegoat. The expositors observe the made unclean: because the goat bore the sins of the congregation. The expositors observe the washing: required before he could come into the camp. The expositors observe the the lesson: the real weight of guilt laid upon the substitute who was made sin for us. The believer is taught to mark the the man, the made unclean, the washing, the the lesson, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that whatever touched the sin-bearing goat was defiled — a token of the real weight of the guilt laid upon the substitute, who was made sin for us.
27:
"And the bullock for the sin offering, and the goat for the sin offering... shall one carry forth without the camp; and they shall burn in the fire their skins, and their flesh, and their dung." The witnesses note the bodies of the sin offerings carried forth and burned outside the camp, their blood having been brought in to make atonement. The witnesses note that Gill notes the one for Aaron and the other for the people, their bodies carried without the camp and burned. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch mark that their blood was brought into the holy place to make atonement, and so the bodies were burned outside. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the sin offerings — the bullock for the priest and the goat for the people; the without the camp — their bodies carried forth and burned with fire; the the reason — their blood having been brought in to make atonement.

The witnesses provide one of the New Testament's plainest types: because the blood was brought into the sanctuary for sin, the bodies were burned outside the camp — wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate (Heb. 13:11-12).

So the witnesses make the verse the bodies burned without: The witnesses note the the sin offerings: the bullock for the priest and the goat for the people. The witnesses note the without the camp: their bodies carried forth and burned with fire. The witnesses note the the reason: their blood having been brought in to make atonement. The expositors observe the the sin offerings: the bullock for the priest and the goat for the people. The expositors observe the without the camp: their bodies carried forth and burned with fire. The expositors observe the the reason: their blood having been brought in to make atonement. The believer is taught to mark the the sin offerings, the without the camp, the the reason, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the body whose blood was brought in for sin was burned outside the camp — the very figure Hebrews applies to Christ, who suffered without the gate.
28:
"And he that burneth them shall wash his clothes, and bathe his flesh in water, and afterward he shall come into the camp." The witnesses note that those who carried and burned the bodies of the sin offerings were likewise defiled and must wash before returning. The witnesses note that Gill notes that these being sin offerings, connected with the sins of men, those concerned in burning them needed washing. The witnesses note that Ellicott joins this with the man who led the goat, both contracting defilement from the atoning victims. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he that burneth — those who carried and burned the bodies; the made unclean — defiled by the atoning victims that bore sin; the washing — required before coming into the camp; the the witness — a repeated token of how truly the people's sin rested upon the victims.

The witnesses agree the same defilement that touched the scapegoat's keeper touched those who burned the sin offerings — the uncleanness of the sin laid on the victims passing to all who handled them; a repeated witness to how real and weighty the transfer of guilt was held to be.

So the witnesses make the verse the defiled burner: The witnesses note the he that burneth: those who carried and burned the bodies. The witnesses note the made unclean: defiled by the atoning victims that bore sin. The witnesses note the washing: required before coming into the camp. The witnesses note the the witness: a repeated token of how truly the people's sin rested upon the victims. The expositors observe the he that burneth: those who carried and burned the bodies. The expositors observe the made unclean: defiled by the atoning victims that bore sin. The expositors observe the washing: required before coming into the camp. The expositors observe the the witness: a repeated token of how truly the people's sin rested upon the victims. The believer is taught to mark the he that burneth, the made unclean, the washing, the the witness, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that those who burned the sin offerings were defiled as the scapegoat's keeper was — the weight of the people's sin resting truly on the victims.
29:
"And this shall be a statute for ever unto you: that in the seventh month, on the tenth day of the month, ye shall afflict your souls, and do no work at all..." The witnesses note the perpetual statute fixing the day — the tenth of the seventh month — with the afflicting of souls and rest from all work. The witnesses note that Gill reads the statute as binding so long as the Aaronic priesthood and Levitical dispensation lasted, until the Messiah came and ended these rites. The witnesses note that Henry notes the afflicting of souls by fasting and humiliation, a day of national repentance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a statute for ever — binding while the Levitical order stood, until Messiah came; the the tenth day — of the seventh month, the fixed Day of Atonement; the afflict your souls — fasting and humiliation, with rest from all work.

The witnesses agree the afflicting of souls is the inward side of the day — the atonement made by blood was to be met by the people's self-humbling and contrition; not that sorrow earned the pardon, but that the pardoned must come humbled, fasting before the God who forgives.

So the witnesses make the verse the appointed day: The witnesses note the a statute for ever: binding while the Levitical order stood, until Messiah came. The witnesses note the the tenth day: of the seventh month, the fixed Day of Atonement. The witnesses note the afflict your souls: fasting and humiliation, with rest from all work. The expositors observe the a statute for ever: binding while the Levitical order stood, until Messiah came. The expositors observe the the tenth day: of the seventh month, the fixed Day of Atonement. The expositors observe the afflict your souls: fasting and humiliation, with rest from all work. The believer is taught to mark the a statute for ever, the the tenth day, the afflict your souls, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the day of pardon was a day of fasting and humbling — the atonement by blood met by the people's contrition, pardon received in lowliness.
30:
"For on that day shall the priest make an atonement for you, to cleanse you, that ye may be clean from all your sins before the LORD." The witnesses note the great promise of the day: atonement made to cleanse the people, that they might be clean from all their sins before the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill sees here the sacrifice of Christ, whose soul was made an offering for sin and whose blood cleanses from all sin, the priest a type of our great High Priest. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the atonement made once a year by the anointed priest in his father's stead. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the atonement made — the priest atoning for the people on that day; the to cleanse you — the end of all the ritual being cleansing; the clean before the LORD — from all sins, standing pure in his presence.

The witnesses agree this verse is the gospel of the day: the end of all the blood and ritual is cleansing — to stand clean from all sins before the LORD; what the yearly atonement could only shadow, the blood of Christ accomplishes, cleansing the conscience from dead works (Heb. 9:14).

So the witnesses make the verse the cleansing promised: The witnesses note the atonement made: the priest atoning for the people on that day. The witnesses note the to cleanse you: the end of all the ritual being cleansing. The witnesses note the clean before the LORD: from all sins, standing pure in his presence. The expositors observe the atonement made: the priest atoning for the people on that day. The expositors observe the to cleanse you: the end of all the ritual being cleansing. The expositors observe the clean before the LORD: from all sins, standing pure in his presence. The believer is taught to mark the atonement made, the to cleanse you, the clean before the LORD, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the whole day aimed at cleansing — to be clean from all sins before the LORD, fulfilled in the blood of Christ that purges the conscience.
31:
"It shall be a sabbath of rest unto you, and ye shall afflict your souls, by a statute for ever." The witnesses note the day a sabbath of rest, with the afflicting of souls, by a perpetual statute. The witnesses note that Gill sees the rest as typical of ceasing from sinful works and from dependence on works of righteousness when a man believes in Christ and his atonement. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the phrase "a sabbath of sabbaths," a day of complete and perfect rest, applied only to the highest holy days. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a sabbath of rest — a sabbath of sabbaths, complete and perfect rest; the afflict your souls — rest from labour joined with self-humbling; the a statute for ever — the perpetual obligation of the day; the the figure — the soul ceasing from its own works to rest in the finished atonement.

The witnesses agree the day joins rest and affliction — rest from labour, yet souls afflicted; the rest figures the soul's ceasing from its own works to rest in atonement, while the affliction keeps it humble; a true sabbath of the heart that finds its repose not in self but in the finished work.

So the witnesses make the verse the sabbath of rest: The witnesses note the a sabbath of rest: a sabbath of sabbaths, complete and perfect rest. The witnesses note the afflict your souls: rest from labour joined with self-humbling. The witnesses note the a statute for ever: the perpetual obligation of the day. The witnesses note the the figure: the soul ceasing from its own works to rest in the finished atonement. The expositors observe the a sabbath of rest: a sabbath of sabbaths, complete and perfect rest. The expositors observe the afflict your souls: rest from labour joined with self-humbling. The expositors observe the a statute for ever: the perpetual obligation of the day. The expositors observe the the figure: the soul ceasing from its own works to rest in the finished atonement. The believer is taught to mark the a sabbath of rest, the afflict your souls, the a statute for ever, the the figure, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the day was both rest and affliction — the soul ceasing from its own works to rest in the atonement, yet kept humble before God.
32:
"And the priest, whom he shall anoint, and whom he shall consecrate to minister in the priest's office in his father's stead, shall make the atonement..." The witnesses note that not Aaron only but each anointed high priest succeeding in his father's stead was to perform this atonement, clad in the holy linen garments. The witnesses note that Gill notes that he whom God should anoint, succeeding in the high priesthood, was to make atonement as Aaron did. The witnesses note that Ellicott renders "the priest who shall be anointed," the duly consecrated successor performing the expiation on the Day of Atonement. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the anointed — each high priest consecrated in his turn; the in his father's stead — succeeding to the office as Aaron's heir; the shall make the atonement — performing the same yearly expiation; the the weakness — many priests in succession, because death suffered them not to continue.

The witnesses agree the law looked beyond Aaron to a perpetual succession of mortal priests, each needing in turn to make the same atonement — the very repetition that Hebrews marks as the weakness of the order, since they were many priests because death suffered them not to continue (Heb. 7:23).

So the witnesses make the verse the succeeding priest: The witnesses note the anointed: each high priest consecrated in his turn. The witnesses note the in his father's stead: succeeding to the office as Aaron's heir. The witnesses note the shall make the atonement: performing the same yearly expiation. The witnesses note the the weakness: many priests in succession, because death suffered them not to continue. The expositors observe the anointed: each high priest consecrated in his turn. The expositors observe the in his father's stead: succeeding to the office as Aaron's heir. The expositors observe the shall make the atonement: performing the same yearly expiation. The expositors observe the the weakness: many priests in succession, because death suffered them not to continue. The believer is taught to mark the anointed, the in his father's stead, the shall make the atonement, the the weakness, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the atonement was committed to a succession of dying priests, each repeating it — the weakness Hebrews sets against the one priest who continues ever.
33:
"And he shall make an atonement for the holy sanctuary... for the tabernacle... and for the altar, and... for the priests, and for all the people of the congregation." The witnesses note the full compass of the atonement: sanctuary, tabernacle, altar, priests, and all the people, every part embraced in the one day's work. The witnesses note that Gill takes the holy sanctuary as the holy of holies, atoned for in the same manner as Aaron did. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott read this as the summary of the whole chapter, the successor performing all as detailed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sanctuary and tabernacle — the holy of holies and the holy place cleansed; the the altar — the place of meeting atoned for; the priests and people — all the congregation embraced in the one work; the the figure — the comprehensive cleansing wrought by Christ for all his own.

The witnesses agree the verse gathers the whole into one view: the most holy place, the holy place, the altar, the priests, and the people — nothing left untouched by the atoning blood; a figure of the comprehensive reach of Christ's atonement, which leaves no part of his church or its worship uncleansed.

So the witnesses make the verse the whole atonement: The witnesses note the sanctuary and tabernacle: the holy of holies and the holy place cleansed. The witnesses note the the altar: the place of meeting atoned for. The witnesses note the priests and people: all the congregation embraced in the one work. The witnesses note the the figure: the comprehensive cleansing wrought by Christ for all his own. The expositors observe the sanctuary and tabernacle: the holy of holies and the holy place cleansed. The expositors observe the the altar: the place of meeting atoned for. The expositors observe the priests and people: all the congregation embraced in the one work. The expositors observe the the figure: the comprehensive cleansing wrought by Christ for all his own. The believer is taught to mark the sanctuary and tabernacle, the the altar, the priests and people, the the figure, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the atonement reached every part — sanctuary, altar, priests, and people — a figure of the comprehensive cleansing wrought by Christ for all his own.
34:
"And this shall be an everlasting statute unto you, to make an atonement for the children of Israel for all their sins once a year. And he did as the LORD commanded Moses." The witnesses note the closing charge: an everlasting statute, atonement for all Israel's sins once a year — and Aaron did as the LORD commanded. The witnesses note that Gill marks this the third time the statute is called everlasting, a law of weight to be strictly kept, and notes Aaron did according to this order when the day came. The witnesses note that Ellicott renders "a statute for ever," noting the threefold repetition within a few verses. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the everlasting statute — the third time so called, a law of weight; the once a year — atonement repeated yearly for all Israel's sins; the he did as commanded — aaron performing all as the LORD ordered.

The witnesses fasten on the words "once a year": the very repetition is the law's confession of its own insufficiency, for the atonement had to be made again every year; whereas Christ, by one offering, has perfected for ever them that are sanctified, entering once into the holy place (Heb. 9:25-26).

So the witnesses make the verse the closing statute: The witnesses note the everlasting statute: the third time so called, a law of weight. The witnesses note the once a year: atonement repeated yearly for all Israel's sins. The witnesses note the he did as commanded: Aaron performing all as the LORD ordered. The expositors observe the everlasting statute: the third time so called, a law of weight. The expositors observe the once a year: atonement repeated yearly for all Israel's sins. The expositors observe the he did as commanded: Aaron performing all as the LORD ordered. The believer is taught to mark the everlasting statute, the once a year, the he did as commanded, and to rest the weight of the day upon the atonement itself. The verse thus records that the atonement made "once a year" had ever to be repeated — the law's own witness to its insufficiency, answered by the one offering of Christ once for all.
